items.title: Fit detection on training data only
items.avoid: Computing global quartiles, means, or percentile clip bounds on train+validation+test together.
items.prefer: Learn bounds from `X_train` (or inner train), persist them, and apply identically at inference.
items.why: Preprocessing must not observe holdout distributions; otherwise evaluation is optimistically biased.
items.example:
```python
lo, hi = X_train[col].quantile([0.01, 0.99])
X_test[col] = X_test[col].clip(lo, hi)
```

items.title: Match method to distribution shape
items.avoid: Defaulting to z-scores on every numeric column without plotting.
items.prefer: Use IQR or MAD-based rules for skewed/heavy-tailed features; consider log transforms before modeling.
items.why: Robust methods fail less often when normality is unrealistic.
items.example: For `revenue`, compare box plots before/after log1p; IQR on log-revenue often aligns better with business intuition.

items.title: Document treatment as part of the model
items.avoid: Silently dropping rows in a notebook cell with no record of thresholds.
items.prefer: Serialize clip bounds, IQR multipliers, and removal flags in the same pipeline object as the estimator.
items.why: Production and audits require reproducible, versioned preprocessing—not one-off notebook steps.
items.example: Wrap `FunctionTransformer` + `SimpleImputer` + model in a single `sklearn.pipeline.Pipeline` saved with joblib.

items.title: Prefer robustness when extremes are real
items.avoid: Clipping all rare high values that define the prediction task.
items.prefer: Use Huber loss, RANSAC, median-based features, or tree ensembles when tails carry signal.
items.why: Capping legitimate extremes teaches the model to ignore the very patterns you need.
items.example: For insurance loss modeling, winsorize only above the 99.5th percentile on train instead of 95th, or use Tweedie/quantile loss.
